text: Hello, this is Roxen Liu from Oakland, California.
This video will demonstrate my current technique for robotic intraperitoneal onlay mesh for small umbilical epigastric incisional or ventral hernias.

A well-planned and executed port placement strategy is critical to success of the robotic IPOM procedure. And this starts with good patient positioning, appropriate abdominal mapping, and insufflation of the abdomen prior to port insertion.

The patient is positioned by sliding them to the ipsilateral edge of the bed, with the ipsilateral arm slung and tucked below the table level. The bed is flexed to increase the distance between the causal margin and the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS), accommodating the vertically pitched robotic instrument.
The surgeon insufflates the abdomen using a Veress needle, preparing the surgical field for the placement of robotic ports.
The surgeon marks the area of coverage by the mesh on the abdominal wall, determining a point approximately ten centimeters away from the mesh edge for reference.
The surgeon positions the camera and dominant hand ports laterally, ensuring they are placed at least ten centimeters away from the mesh edge, ideally fifteen to twenty centimeters.

The surgeon positions the robotic ports approximately six to seven centimeters apart, adjusting the left lower quadrant port inward and upward to prevent collision with the thigh during the procedure.
The surgeon begins the operation by reducing the incarcerated omentum, with assistance from the bedside assistant.
In this patient there was lack of infraumbilical posterior rectus sheath and peritoneum so the tap operation was not a good option.
The surgeon removes the falciform fat to improve mesh apposition to the fascia. If significant umbilical ligament fat is present, it would be similarly excised.
The surgeon divides the ligamentum teres, ensuring they are operating within the correct anatomical plane.
For those of you that do not perform much tap repairs, this is a great operation to start overcoming that learning curve.

The surgeon removes the median fat pad and carefully dissects some of the peritoneum from the posterior rectus sheath. The transversalis fascia is separated from the anterior structures to preserve a robust peritoneal layer.
I also use a falciform fat later on to cover my mesh to avoid suturing into the diastatic linea alba along the mesh periphery. And I will explain this later.
The surgeon observes a two-centimeter rectus diastasis in the patient. The fascial defect is closed using a running V-lock 180 suture, visibly bringing the edges of the fascia together.
This closure can be done either vertically or transversely. And this is a six inch suture for these small hernias. I find that six inch sutures are more than adequate in length.
The surgeon instructs the bedside assistant to push down on the umbilical skin while carefully taking a small bite of the hernia sac to invert the umbilicus.

The surgeon lays sutures without tightening them, facilitating the grasping of the umbilical stock through a small hernia defect. The suture is run back at least two throws, sometimes covering the entire length of the defect, while maintaining insufflation pressure.

The surgeon uses a Bard Ventralight ST mesh, pre-placing a V-Loc 180 suture in the center to suspend the mesh towards the ceiling. The suture is then threaded back through the mesh onto the coated side.
The surgeon secures the mesh on their side by suturing it towards themselves, ensuring at least a four-centimeter overlap for the smaller defect.
The surgeon uses the same suture to perform a baseball stitch circumferentially around the defect, advancing the stitch with intervals of slightly less than a centimeter.
The surgeon uses a twelve-inch V-Loc suture to begin suturing in a counterclockwise direction, approaching the area of the diastasis.

I'm a firm believer that the twelve o'clock stitch through the diastatic linea alba with traditional laparoscopic eye palm and even robotic eye palm leads to a higher recurrence rate because the diastasis is weaker and the suture in that area is more likely to pull through. When I get to the diastasis, I will actually move a little bit more inward on the mesh, meaning away from the edge of the mesh. This way, if the suture were to pull through, there would still be mesh covering the defect in the diastasis. Later, I will use a falciform to cover this area of the mesh that is not secured down.
This is a small modification I make from experience with laparoscopic incisional hernia repairs for many years and seeing recurrences at this twelve o'clock position quite often.

The surgeon continues suturing around the periphery of the mesh after passing the diastasis area, securing the central part of the mesh to the abdominal wall to minimize the pocket and reduce seroma formation. The video is sped up to show the straightforward suturing process.
The surgeon alternates between suturing techniques, going from flesh to mesh and mesh to flesh, as they secure the mesh to the abdominal wall.
The surgeon secures the falciform ligament to the abdominal wall using absorbable braided sutures, covering the portion of the mesh that was not secured at the edge.
The surgeon demonstrates clear visualization of the mesh, particularly the ipsilateral edge, facilitated by effective port placement. The mesh appears adequately sized for the surgical field.

Should I have needed a fifteen centimeter mesh, I would have placed my ports even more lateral and provided myself with the same exposure and distance from the areas where I needed to work. So this concludes this video. Thank you for watching and I will produce a similar video for a tap repair for such a small hernia too. Thank you.
